beats.vo: Meet ReGrade, from Curtail. It catches behavioral regressions in your API — the subtle changes in behavior that slip past your tests and reach your users.

It's built for the way you build software now — in a loop with an AI agent. You change the code. The agent uses the ReGrade MCP to replay your previous version's real traffic against the new build. ReGrade logs every behavior or performance difference between the two versions. The agent tags each one as intended, or an unintended regression. And you use that summary to converge on a fix — faster, and more securely. Let's walk through one turn of that loop, on a tiny example.

First, a quick one-time setup — and it's free. Create an account and grab an API key. Install the sensor. Then add the ReGrade tools to Claude Code with two commands, and connect them once. Now we're ready.
We start two versions of a tiny orders API. You'll record traffic against version one, and replay it against version two.

The sensor proxies real traffic and captures it. In a second terminal, we drive that traffic with the demo's included script — a login, then a few reads. When we stop the proxy, it prints a recording ID.

Now we replay the recording against version two. ReGrade compares every response and finds twenty-two differences. But most of them come from one dynamic field the replay didn't adapt to. Let's ask Claude to explain.

Back in Claude Code — or any other MCP-connected harness, with the model of your choice — we type a request in plain English: walk me through my latest ReGrade replay. Claude runs the ReGrade tools for us, and we see a wall of Status Code 401s. That status code means unauthorized — the server is refusing the request. Here's why: every time you log in, the service hands you a brand-new token, like a fresh key card. Our recording still sends the old card, but version two already retired it, so every request is turned away, and the real order data hides behind the errors. Here's the important distinction: this isn't a bug — but it isn't noise either. Noise is a value that always differs, like a timestamp. This is the replay reusing a stale token that version two has already rotated — it just needs to adapt to that dynamic field.

To make the replay adapt, we type: can you clear these 401 errors so I can see what changed? Claude suggests two small rules — one learns the fresh token from each login, the other swaps it into the request before it's replayed, so a valid token goes out instead of the stale one. It waits for our okay, so we type: looks right, create them.

We replay once more, this time with the profile, so the fresh token is substituted as the requests go out. And the profile is saved with your app — every future replay can reuse it, so you only map a dynamic field once.

Finally, we type: analyze the new replay. Now that the replay adapts the token, the 401s are gone, and one real difference remains: the order total dropped from forty-six twenty to forty-two. Version two quietly stopped adding sales tax — a bug that would undercharge every order. ReGrade caught it from the traffic alone, with no test written for it.

Ready to try it on your own service? Create a free account at curtail dot com. No credit card, no expiration, and free every month for individual use. And you can run this exact demo yourself at github dot com slash Curtail dash Inc slash hello dash ReGrade.
